™
JB
PART I:
INTRODUCTION TO
THE STRAIGHT LINE SYSTEM
PART 2

-- 1 of 11 --

ESTABLISHING RAPPORT
made the case previously that establishing yourself as an expert in your field
is critical to moving down the straight line toward the close. One of the ways
you’ll do this is by establishing rapport.
Unfortunately, this word rapport is one of the most misunderstood in the English
language. What most salespeople see as rapport, most prospects see as repulsive.
This pseudo-rapport actually drives prospects away.
Remember, your prospect wants you to be an expert in your field. No one wants
to deal with a novice. Experts take control of the conversation by asking pertinent
questions in a logical order. Rapport is established when your prospects come to
the realization that you know what you’re talking about (or asking about). They
like you because they have a real sense that you have their best interests in mind.
And when that happens they’re more likely to open up to you and share their
needs, values and pain points, all of which you’ll need for a successful close.
Novices on the other hand, allow prospects to take control by following them off
on tangents that have nothing to do with the sales presentation. Novices see this
as a way to get prospects to like them. However, when prospects are in control
they have little respect for the salesperson’s ability to meet their immediate needs
or solve their immediate problems. They find this repulsive!
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com
I

-- 2 of 11 --

JORDAN BELFORT
Moreover, experts are in demand, which means time is a very precious commodity
for them. So experts stay on task and prospects quickly come to appreciate their
single-minded focus. They readily answer the questions asked of them because
they don’t want to waste an expert’s time! That is a sign of respect.
Wrapping Your Package
Speaking of respect, this would be a good time to elaborate on a subject I touched
on in Part 1, that is, your appearance. People do judge a book by its cover and
with only 1/4 of one second to make the right impression, you’ve got to look your
best.
The man who wears a suit and tie will immediately be perceived as a person of
power. For a woman it’s a pantsuit or skirt, with the hem not more than an inch
above the knee. Refrain from wearing excessive makeup, jewelry, perfume or
cologne. Too much and you undermine your own credibility.
There’s more, like facial hair. A clean cut gives the best impression, but a neatly
trimmed mustache and a closely cropped beard are acceptable. Hair styles should
also tend toward the conservative side.
The point in all this is that your “package” should not in any way distract your
prospect or detract from your message.
Of course there are cultural or environmental exceptions. If you sell Harleys, a
suit and tie would look out of place and a beard may be perfectly acceptable.
Likewise, if you sell jewelry, it might seem odd if you wore none at all.
The bottom line is that whatever your sales environment, neatness and attention
to detail are things any prospect will appreciate, and, in fact, will expect.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 3 of 11 --

JORDAN BELFORT
Remember, your prospect will be sizing you up on both a conscious and
unconscious level. That means you want to avoid anything that’ll have your
prospect second-guessing your abilities or your intentions.
Then There’s Body Language
Just like there are rules for dress, there are rules for handshakes, proper stance and
respecting your prospect’s “space.”
Let’s begin with your stance. Men should never face off with other men because it
can be viewed as confrontational by your prospect. Think ice hockey, gunfights of
the Old West or even a bare knuckles street fight and you get the picture. It’s one
of those things hard wired into the male psyche. So men should stand at a slight
angle to other men. Simple as that.
On the other hand, women want men to face them, but the caveat here is that
men should keep their hands above waist level where women can see them.
Perhaps surprisingly, women also want other women to stand at an angle. It seems
the gentler sex is just as competitive.
And space? For Westerners, our comfort zone starts at a minimum of two-and-a-
half to three feet. Asians on the other hand are more tolerant at a mere six inches.
Handshakes have their own set of rules and while a soft, mushy hand is definitely
out of the question, so is the bone crushing variety. Interestingly enough, the soft
handshake from a prospect may indicate a feeling of superiority, as in, “you mean
so little to me I can’t be bothered to shake your hand.”
But remember, your objective is to establish rapport, so you’ll want to match your
prospect’s handshake as closely as possible. Your brain will react instantly to the
pressure being applied and you’ll equal it. Maintain eye contact while in a
handshake just in case your prospect tries to pull a fast one and increases the
pressure. You don’t want to get into a squeeze-off contest, so use your eye contact
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 4 of 11 --

JORDAN BELFORT
to send the message that you’re not intimidated, even if your prospect goes into
extra squeeze mode.
Stare-off contests are just as undesirable. You’ll come off a bit scary if your eyes are
constantly on your prospect. Studies show the optimum percentage of direct eye
contact in a face-to-face meeting to be 72%. But you won’t need a mental
stopwatch. Maintain eye contact out of genuine interest rather than mechanical
necessity and you’ll do just fine.
Listen With Your Whole Body
Genuine interest is also shown by your listening habits. Your prospect can actually
see you listening by the way you move your body, your facial expressions, your
smile and those little groans and grunts of concern, affirmation and the like.
A well-timed nod, smile, raised eyebrow, tilted head accompanied by an aha!, yup!
or got it! are proof enough that you’re paying attention and that you care. Got it?
Body language, especially facial expressions, convey a host of emotions from glee
to concern to empathy to agreement. Try them out in front of a mirror. Seriously!
Stand in front of a mirror, let an emotional thought come to mind and see how
your face responds. It’s helpful to see what your prospect sees. It’s also helpful to
begin training yourself to detect emotions in others by their body language.
By the way, body language is transmitted through the phone too. You’ll sound
different to your prospect slumped in your chair (passive) compared to sitting
upright (active) to sitting on the edge with your elbows on the desk (intense) to
standing upright with your chest pumped up (commanding). Also important are
those little sounds of acknowledgement. They’re critical to maintaining rapport.
So use them, but don’t overdo it.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 5 of 11 --

JORDAN BELFORT
Matching and Mirroring
When best friends are together for even a few moments they begin to match and
mirror each other. That is, they react the same way to stimuli, they move in
unison, they complete each other’s thoughts. They’re like two peas in a pod. It’s all
human nature. They don’t need to put any effort into this. Their pre-established
rapport makes matching and mirroring possible.
The flip side is also true. You can use matching and mirroring to establish and
maintain rapport on an unconscious level.
The two are nearly identical in that they require you to do what your prospect
does. He leans in and speaks softly. You lean in and speak softly. She tilts her head
slightly. You tilt your head slightly.
The difference is that mirroring is an instantaneous reaction while matching has a
time delay. I don’t advise the use of mirroring very often. It gets creepy real fast.
Matching however, waiting five to ten seconds before making your move, is much
more subtle and can be very effective. That’s because your movements are sending
messages to your prospect’s unconscious mind that you’re like him or her. Because
we’re more at ease around people who are like us, rapport grows rapidly.
But that’s only half the story and not even the best half. See, with matching and
mirroring you’re following your prospect’s lead, building rapport step-by-step
through physical movement.
Pacing and Leading
They’re kissin’ cousins to matching and mirroring. The difference is that now
you’re entering your prospect’s world on an emotional, rather than a physical
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 6 of 11 --

JORDAN BELFORT
level. You’ll still make good use of body language, but now you’ll also employ
effective tonality. Remember tonality?
Pacing is like Spock’s mind meld where you meet your prospects at whatever level
they’re at. You see eye-to-eye emotionally. You’re totally in synch. Sympatico.
He’s pissed because his office products supplier keeps dropping the ball. So you
get just as pissed and empathize with his plight. He throws himself back in his
chair and grips the armrests. You smack your forehead in an I-can’t believe-they-
did-that-to-you moment. She’s upset because her car broke down for the second
time this week. You’re right there on her emotional level, free-flowing with
empathy.
Leading is taking your prospects out of their negative emotional states and
moving them into your positive emotional state. But it’ s more than that. It’s
taking any prospect and leading him along the straight line to the close.
Gathering Intelligence
This is the Holy Grail of any great close: the ability to dig deep enough to discover
what’s motivating your prospect. It goes far beyond wants and needs because at
the heart of those two drivers is pain.
If you’re selling life insurance, pain comes in the form of your prospect’s concern
for his family’s future financial needs. If you’re selling cars pain comes in the form
of driving a rustbucket that breaks down in the middle of rush hour traffic. If you
sell power tools, pain comes in the form of time wasted hand sawing wood for a
weekend project.
Every prospect has at least one pain point and often several that you’ll need to
uncover and address. There will be times when the pain has existed for so long
your prospects have become numb to it. They deny it even exists. It just so
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 7 of 11 --

JORDAN BELFORT
happens that the Straight Line System is perfectly suited for bringing these pain
points to the surface.
It all goes back to asking the right questions in the right order. You begin with
big-picture questions which are general in nature and thus less invasive. As you
build rapport and your prospect opens up, you can begin asking more specific
questions.
Keep in mind that you’re not selling anything yet. Imagine going to the doctor
and before you can say one word about your pain, he writes out a prescription for
you. How much faith would you put in this doctor? You’d be wondering, “does
this guy even care about me?”
Make sure your prospect senses that you care. Look for the pain points. Look for
values and beliefs as well. You can use those to emphasize the pain. Let’s go back
to the car sales scenario.
Pain for your prospect is the embarrassment and inconvenience of driving an ugly,
unreliable car. Then you discover your prospect works for a company that's
looking to downsize. How secure will his job be if he spends half the day on the
side of the road waiting for a tow truck? He’s got two teenage sons. How much
more embarrassment do they feel when their buddies’ parents all drive brand
spankin’ new cars?
So bring the pain to the surface if it isn’t already apparent. Then keep that pain
fresh in your prospect’s mind as you continue gathering intelligence.
Decision Time
Okay, so you’ve built rapport and gathered intelligence. You’re fully aware of your
prospect’s pain points and there’s no doubt in your mind that your prospect can
feel that pain right now. Now what?
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 8 of 11 --

JORDAN BELFORT
Well, that depends on whether your prospect is closeable or not. What if you
don’t have a remedy? What if you do and your prospect can’t afford it? The right
thing to do is admit the truth. There’s nothing you can do. But …
If you know a competitor who can help, introduce your prospect to that
competitor. You’ll end up with something that, in the long run, may be worth
more than a sale. You’ll have the good will of the prospect and the competitor.
Hey, what goes around comes around.
If, on the other hand, you’re totally convinced you can close your prospect then
it’s time to move forward with your presentation. You need to very smoothly
transition from the gathering-intelligence phase to the presentation phase. One
sentence will do the trick and it’ll go something like this …
“Well, John, let me say this: Based on everything you just told me, this program is
definitely a perfect fit for you. Let me tell you why …”
Substitute whatever you want for “this program” and do add emphasis to that
word “definitely.”
Let’s review what you’ve learned today.
Rapport means open dialogue between you and your prospect that is confined to
the sales issue.
Rapport allows you to:
1. 	Establish your expertise.
2. 	Maintain control of the conversation.
3. 	Gather intelligence.
4. 	Show you care.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 9 of 11 --

JORDAN BELFORT
Appearance
1. 	Proper appearance for the man:
- 	Suit and tie give perception of power.
- 	Refrain from wearing excessive jewelry and cologne.
- 	No facial hair or neatly trimmed mustache and beard.
- 	Conservative hairstyle.
2. 	Proper appearance for the woman:
- 	Pantsuit or skirt with hem no higher than just above the knee.
- 	Refrain from wearing excessive makeup, jewelry and perfume.
- 	Conservative hairstyle.
3. 	Rules may vary depending on sales environment.
4. 	Neatness and attention to detail are always welcome.
Body Language
1. 	Man/man and woman/woman should stand at a slight angle to each other.
2. 	Man/woman should be face-to-face with man’s hands above the waist.
3. 	Distance should be no less than 2 1/2 feet for Westerners. Asians are
comfortable down to 6 inches.
4. 	Your handshake should match that of your prospect in style and grip, while
maintaining eye contact.
5. 	Overall, eye contact should be maintained around 72% of the time during
your meeting.
6. 	Signal your interest and attention to your prospect through your body
language.
7. 	Match your prospect’s movements to maintain unconscious rapport.
8. 	Pace prospects in their world, then lead them into your world.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 10 of 11 --

JORDAN BELFORT
Wrap Up
1. 	Gather intelligence by asking the right questions in the right order. Stay on
topic and begin with big-picture questions. Narrow your focus as you
proceed.
2. 	You’ll want to uncover wants, needs, don’t wants, pain points, beliefs and
values.
3. 	Is your prospect closeable?
4. 	If not, try to offer alternatives including your own competitors.
5. 	If yes, then transition to your presentation.
© 2015 by Jordan Belfort. All rights reserved. 	www.JordanBelfort.com

-- 11 of 11 --
